Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Dividend Growth Fund (FDGFX) is a diversified domestic equity strategy with a large-cap core orientation. The fund seeks capital appreciation.
  • The fund invests in a mix of large- and mid-cap stocks that have favorable prospects to sustainably pay and grow dividends over time.
  • Our investment philosophy centers on comparing price and value. We believe price will converge with value over time in a competitive market. Quality is an integral part of our assessment of value.
  • We also believe that companies with a history of growing dividends demonstrate superior risk-adjusted returns over the course of a market cycle.
  • A disciplined approach combining fundamental analysis, quality, valuation and accelerating capital return can help lead to outperformance over time.

Performance Review

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 5.09%, topping the 2.66% advance of the benchmark S&P 500® index.

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500® index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and two interest-rate reductions by the Federal Reserve. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large- caps had only a slight advantage over small-caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

During the quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI, as media & entertainment stocks were up 9%. All other groups lagged the index. Real estate (-3%) and utilities (-1%), small components of the S&P 500, each lost ground, while consumer staples finished at roughly breakeven. Materials, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks all gained roughly 1%, as did information technology, which represented 35% of the index in Q4. Financials (+2%) reflected a strong showing by banks (+7%) but weakness among insurers (-1%), and energy (+2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil.

Turning to the fund, stock selection was the primary driver of the fund's excess performance over the benchmark, especially in information technology, industrials and communication services. Overweight exposure to Alphabet contributed most at the stock level. The shares rose about 29% the past three months, as the parent of the Google search engine continued to execute on its artificial intelligence strategy while producing solid financial results across its other businesses. Investors were especially positive on the firm's fast-growing cloud-computing division, while the advertising and search segments also showed strength. In November, Google debuted an updated version of its AI model, Gemini, that company executives said represented a "massive jump" in reasoning and coding ability. We added a bit to this position, given the strong fundamental backdrop.

An out-of-benchmark stake in South Korea-headquartered SK Hynix was also among the fund's top relative contributors. The stock gained roughly 83% the past three months, rallying amid strong demand for its digital memory solutions. In a third-quarter conference call with analysts, the company said that all of its memory products – including DRAM, NAND and high-bandwidth memory chips – were sold out through 2026. To address the shortage, management said it would increase capital spending to meet demand, prioritizing technology migration and infrastructure build-out. We increased this position in view of the extraordinarily robust demand for the firm's products.

Conversely, an underweight in health care detracted in a quarter where that sector led all others. In stock-specific terms, overweighting Paycom Software (-23%) detracted most. The company offers a cloud-based human resources platform targeted to U.S. small and mid-sized companies. The stock declined partly due to broader weakness in software stocks amid concern that AI might usurp much of the functionality of software applications at a lower cost. Additionally, the company's Q3 financial results were mixed. While revenue grew by 9% year over year, earnings growth decelerated amid weaker operating margins. Several analyst downgrades also weighed on the shares. We reduced this position but maintained an overweight, given our favorable long-term view of the company.

A sizable underweight in Apple also hurt, as the stock advanced about 7% this quarter. The stock was bolstered by strong results for the quarter ending September 27. Revenue was up 8%, while earnings per share rose 13%, both surpassing consensus expectations, buoyed by robust demand for the new iPhone 17 lineup, as well as record sales in Apple's services segment. In his October 30 update, CEO Tim Cook projected growth of 10% to 12% for the holiday-season quarter. We continued to see better opportunities elsewhere.■

Outlook and Positioning

We remain optimistic about the prospects for generative artificial intelligence. We continue to believe the current breakthroughs in large language and other transformer and diffusion-based models will have massive implications for how developed economies function. The impact will be at least as big as the development of the transistor or the World Wide Web, in our opinion. Among companies positioned to benefit from AI, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Alphabet were some of the fund's largest overweights as of December 31.

Within the cyclically driven earnings bucket, a key theme for the fund is strengthening commercial aviation, which is one of the very few end markets that has not yet recovered to its pre-pandemic levels of production, despite a robust recovery in air travel. A key holding here is Boeing (BA), the fund's No. 1 overweight at year-end. The company is still working to resolve some longstanding production issues. With that said, Boeing's backlog was down to 10.6 years at the end of 2025, from 17.2 years in 2024, when its delivery numbers were very low. Additionally, the firm raised roughly $24 billion of capital in the fall of 2024 by selling additional shares of stock, helping to strengthen its balance sheet. All told, we're anticipating better times for Boeing.

Elsewhere in the industrials sector, GE Vernova (GEV) was another meaningful overweight at year-end. The company is a global leader in the electric power industry. Its largest division makes gas turbines, which are used to generate electricity. Amid the global push toward electrification and decarbonization – including supplying the world's increased power needs due to the advent of generative AI – Vernova appears well positioned.

The energy sector was a significant overweight as of year-end. The rationale here is reasonable valuations, corporate policies about returning capital to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks, and the steady albeit slow growth in global energy consumption. In this sector, the fund is focused on mission-critical infrastructure providers and exploration & production firms with low decline rates and predictable capital spending policies. Individual energy positions of note at quarter end included Enterprise Products Partners (EPD).

Information technology was our largest sector underweight at the end of Q4. Within technology, the portfolio held a sizable underweight in technology hardware & equipment, mainly because of Apple, mentioned earlier. Although the stock did well the past three months, we remain cautious about the longer-term outlook for it, given ongoing tariff concerns and intense competition in the smartphone market. The fund also remained underweight the software & services segment but overweight the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry.

Elsewhere, the fund is underweight defensive, staples-like stocks, but we're watching for opportunities to lessen that underweight in this somewhat neglected area of the market.

Overall, top individual overweights at the end of December included Boeing, Alphabet and GE Vernova. Top non-benchmark positions were Taiwan Semiconductor Company, Brookfield and Allison Transmission Holdings (ALSN). The top-three underweights were Apple, Amazon.com and Tesla (TSLA).

As always, we thank you for your confidence in us and in Fidelity's investment-management capabilities.■

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R- Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Microsoft: Be Greedy When Others Are Fearful
data.description:
Reasons to continue buying MSFT

I have covered Microsoft (MSFT) only once in my career as a Seeking Alpha author, and it was a bullish call as of October 27. The timing of my previous call coincided with nearly peak AI optimism, so it is no wonder that as of today my bullishness did not age well. Microsoft is currently 24% cheaper than in late October 2025 despite growing financial metrics and expanding AI foothold. The market is currently in extreme fear mode, which means that the stock price might continue stagnating for a while. However, I believe it is a great opportunity to buy the dip because "be greedy when others are fearful" usually works well when we speak about high-quality companies.

Let's start with the company's valuation. Microsoft is currently way cheaper than its historical multiples across the board. Moreover, the market prices factor in quite low premiums for Microsoft in terms of the P/E ratio as well as the price/cash flow. A narrow premium compared to the sector could have been justified if Microsoft demonstrated revenue growth and profitability in line with peers. However, this is not applicable because Microsoft's revenue is expected to grow faster than the sector by 50% in the current fiscal year, while its profitability is miles ahead of the sector.

Deep discounts compared to the company's own historical averages could have been justified if Microsoft's revenue and EPS growth were decelerating rapidly. However, this is also not true because the company's revenue growth is expected to remain within the same range of approximately 15%-16% at least until the end of the 2020s. The bottom line's CAGR is expected to be slightly higher than the revenue growth rate, which indicates that Wall Street expects Microsoft to maintain its solid operating leverage.

Thus, Microsoft's multiples should return closer to historical averages once the market sentiment improves to at least neutral or even moderately optimistic. As a result, the fair share price is the historical forward P/E of 31.6 multiplied by the current fiscal year's EPS forecast of $16.73. The product of this multiplication is nearly $530, indicating that Microsoft is currently undervalued by more than 30%.

I have high conviction in Wall Street's revenue and growth EPS estimates for Microsoft because of several qualitative factors. Microsoft's Azure still commands a massive 20%+ market share in the accelerating cloud infrastructure market, which makes it a cornerstone of the AI era. Having such a strong market share means that Microsoft's cloud business has a huge customer base, and developing new AI capabilities via the Copilot product helps the company to expand its cross-selling potential. For instance, the company recently introduced a $99/month AI-powered software suite to boost adoption of Copilot. Analysts from Morgan Stanley believe that this upgrade significantly boosts Microsoft's foothold in the AI era.

Besides the increasing potential for cross-selling, Microsoft is also entering entirely new AI-related verticals. The latest reports suggest that Microsoft is expanding its AI capabilities into the healthcare vertical with the introduction of Copilot Health. Based on what the report describes, it will be a comprehensive ecosystem bridging together healthcare (records, visit summaries, medication lists, test results) and fitness/wellness (activity levels, sleep patterns, records from smart devices like Oura or Fitbit). Such an offering looks very promising considering the secular trend of people becoming more health conscious after the COVID-19 pandemic. This strategic move also aligns well with the regulatory environment, as the U.S. government is striving to make the country's healthcare more digitalized. Overall, the global healthcare market is worth almost $9 trillion, while its digitalization is still quite low across the world. With its strong positioning in cloud infrastructure plus AI, vast expertise in digitalization, and capacity to invest in innovation, Microsoft is well-positioned to capitalize on this new healthcare-related vertical.

Finally, the technical analysis suggests that all the pessimism is already factored in by the market. The share price has been consolidated around $400 for more than a month, meaning that peak pressure from bears is likely in the rearview mirror. The last time MSFT was consolidating around $400 was in spring 2025, after which a swift rally to $500 happened just within 4 months. Considering Microsoft's strong financial performance and vital bullish qualitative developments, I think that the same calendar Q2 rally can happen again.

Reasons to avoid MSFT

However, there is one big difference in spring 2026 compared to spring 2025. There is currently a large military operation in the Middle East, which pushes energy prices much higher. In such an environment of elevated inflation risks for the U.S. economy, the Fed might switch back to a hawkish stance. A tighter monetary policy is usually bad for valuations of growth stocks because higher interest rates undermine the discounted value of future cash flow. President Trump does not expect the military operation to last long, meaning that the current spike in energy prices is likely to be unsustainable. However, it is important to understand that Iran is a large country with a quite powerful military. We already saw multiple times from the news that Iran is ready to strike the energy infrastructure of neighboring countries in the Gulf, which might disrupt global energy production or exports. Higher energy prices for longer will almost inevitably lead to a more hawkish Fed because the U.S. labor market is still quite strong. Since the labor market and inflation are the two key metrics for the Fed under its dual mandate, a relatively low unemployment rate means that there is some room for interest rate hikes.

Besides geopolitics and interest rates, there is a large business-specific risk for Microsoft. Microsoft is substantially ahead of Google in terms of cloud infrastructure market share. However, the search giant is also a relentless innovator, and its Gemini 3.1 LLM is really successful and is rapidly gaining popularity. Google also has vast financial resources to invest in R&D and M&A, which is a notable fundamental threat for Microsoft. Recent publications from BNP Paribas suggest that ChatGPT is losing its market share not only to Gemini but to Anthropic's Claude as well. While Microsoft's Copilot has already shifted from "ChatGPT-only" to a multi-LLM setup, it appears that the "Microsoft+OpenAI" narrative is still very strong for the market. Therefore, if OpenAI continues losing its edge in the LLM space, it might weigh on investor sentiment around MSFT.

The verdict

Microsoft looks too cheap to ignore, especially considering its rapidly strengthening foothold in AI and stellar financial performance. The current share price of $400 looks like an extremely strong support level, meaning that downside risks are likely low. Due to soaring energy prices, some near-term volatility might persist. However, as a long-term investor, I prefer to rely more on fundamentals than on sentiment. My rating for MSFT remains the same, a Strong Buy.

data.title: Corteva Splitting Up: One Preferred Keeps Its Strong Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

While I really dislike investing in uncertainty, and Corteva, Inc. (CTVA) having announced they are dividing into two companies definitely qualifies. I reviewed both of its preferred stocks last fall, as owning them would diversify a portfolio’s allocation away from financial issuers. Covered again here will be:

  • Corteva, Inc., $3.50 Series Cumulative Preferred Stock (CTA.PR.A).
  • Corteva, Inc., $4.50 Series Cumulative Preferred Stock (CTA.PR.B).

I contacted the company and was told the preferred stocks will be the responsibility of the new Corteva company, not the spinoff part. That also aligns with the fact that the old DuPont company was the original issuer of both preferred stocks. As then, and despite not being able to analyze the financial statements of the part retaining ownership, I’m maintaining the Strong Buy rating on CTA-B and lowering CTA-A to a Buy based on the current yield spread between the issues.

Corteva reviewed

Seeking Alpha describes this current company as (edited):

Corteva, Inc. operates in the agriculture business. It operates through two segments, Seed and Crop Protection. The Seed segment develops and supplies advanced germplasm and traits that produce optimum yield for farms. It offers trait technologies that enhance resistance to weather, disease, insects, and herbicides used to control weeds, as well as food and nutritional characteristics.

The Crop Protection segment offers products that protect against weeds, insects and other pests, and diseases, as well as enhances crop health above and below ground through nitrogen management and seed-applied technologies.

The company operates in the United States, Canada, Latin America, the Asia Pacific, Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Corteva, Inc. was incorporated in 2018 and is headquartered in Indianapolis, Indiana.

Breakup news

From the news release announcing they are dividing breakup:

Indianapolis, Ind., October 1 – Global agriculture technology leader Corteva announced today that its Board of Directors has unanimously approved a plan to separate the company into two independent, publicly traded companies, one comprising its current Crop Protection business (“New Corteva”) and the other comprising its current Seed business (“SpinCo”).

  • New Corteva to be a market leader differentiated by its innovative solutions, including biologicals, and focus on operational excellence
  • SpinCo to be a leader in advanced genetics and build upon a century-long track record of customer loyalty, elite breeding, market leadership and ﬁnancial strength
  • Tax-free transaction expected to be completed in the second half of 2026

Source: Corteva: press release.

A recent announcement from Corteva said the split is now targeted for the last quarter of this year. Form 10 will be filed with the SEC in the first half of this year, which provides the pro-forma statements for both parts.

First are the Seed results for the past two years.

Now for Crop protection results.

The part staying as Corteva will have 44% of the estimated sales of the existing company, with the spinoff getting 56%. According to this article, the pro forma statements won’t be released until 2026. Due to concerns that the parts staying with the new Corteva are the slower growing businesses, Moody’s downgraded the debt from A3 to Baa1.

Even assuming only 44% of the current Total Common Equity ($25.9B) remains, covering the small amount of preferreds outstanding, even after paying off the LT debt ($1.7B), should not be a concern.

Here is an overview of the parts staying with the new Corteva. I counted that they sell these products and services in over 70 countries on six continents. Of course, that might drop after the spinoff.

Next, we see the breakdown and results by region.

Currently, international sales account for 63% of the total, with Asia's small exposure providing a potential source for major future growth. That said, unless things change, Corteva appears to be in a slow-growing business based on recent organic growth percentages. Another Seeking Alpha contributor gave his spin on what the post-split companies should look like and their investment thesis for both parts (article link).

Analyzing the preferreds

The table compares the key features of each preferred stock.

Factor A B
Size (shares) 1.67MM .7MM
Coupon 3.5% 4.5%
Call date any time any time
Call price $102 $120
Liquidation preference $100 $100
Price in October $54.14 $68.36
Yield then 6.46% 6.58%
Price now $55.25 $68.33
Yield now 6.33% 6.59%

Analysis

It is my belief that both issues were originally issued to du Pont family members as an income source, thus funding their company without diluting their voting control. The issues carry an investment-grade rating of BBB, and dividends qualify for the preferential tax rate. Dividends are cumulative. The low coupon and high Call prices should minimize the Call risk. While pro forma numbers are unknown at this time, I believe after the breakup there should be plenty of common equity to cover both preferred stocks.

Above I mentioned there is plenty of equity to repay the preferreds. Using the same statement assumption, net income is over 40X needed to pay the preferred stock dividends. As a safe choice to diversify a portfolio full of financial institution-issued preferred stocks, both still deserve a place in one's preferred stock portfolio allocation.

Due to the different price movements, currently CTA-B keeps its Strong Buy rating. I drop CTA-A to just a Buy as the indication of my preference.

Portfolio strategy

Compared to the large universe of preferred stocks to pick from, ones like those covered here have several advantages within that realm:

  • They are fixed rate, a good property when rates are declining.
  • They have low coupons, lowering the odds of being Called as rates decline.
  • They add diversity because the issuer isn’t a financial institution.
  • Unless the new entity drastically changes the capitalization, the coverage ratio equates to no default risk.

For those benefits, the investor accepts a lower, but reasonable, yield versus what many mREIT issues offer or some large banks. The uncertainty of what the new balance sheet will look like needs to be taken into consideration in any due diligence about holding the Corteva preferred stocks.

Final thought

In case readers wonder why I do not own CTA-B with its Strong Buy rating, the reason is the same as why I didn’t own DuPont while employed. In both cases, that company was/is a prime income source (paycheck, now pension). While I see no ownership risk in these preferreds, my cautious nature opposes double sourcing. The same logic keeps a lid on my exposure to any one company’s preferreds or debt issues.

data.title: The War Rages On; Equities And Bonds Don't Like It
data.description:
The war and rhetoric around it keep investors on edge, even though WTI and Brent are consolidating now after reaching marginal new four-day highs. The greenback is firm against the G10 currencies. Disappointing UK January GDP has weighed on sterling, whose 0.65% loss leads the majors. The proximity of the JPY160 level may be encouraging a more cautious tone, leaving the yen as the best performer, only off around 0.1%.

The unknown risks over the weekend may help keep greenback supported in North America today. The Dollar Index is up 1.2% this week as the North American session gets underway. Last week, it rose 1.4%. The Dollar Index is at highest level since last November. The average price of retail gasoline in the US is more than 4% this week after a 16% rally last week. Mortgage rates are at 11-month highs.

Prices

G10

• The euro traded poorly yesterday and approached Tuesday’s low slightly above $1.1505. It still posted its lowest settlement since last November. Follow-through selling today has knocked it to slightly below $1.1435, its lowest level since last August. It has recovered to around $1.1470 in late European morning turnover. The technical tone is poor, and a stronger recovery may be held at bay by the heightened uncertainties over the weekend.

• Yesterday, for the third consecutive session, the greenback settled near session highs against the yen. It traded above the high from January 23, when reports indicate that the Federal Reserve checked foreign exchange rates on behalf of the US Treasury. The gains were extended to almost JPY159.70 today, the best level since July 2024. While we expect the market to turn a bit cautious as the psychologically important JPY160 level is approached, we think the risk of intervention is minimal, given the orderliness of the market, the range-bound volatility, and the fact that it has been a largely orderly dollar advance.

• Sterling was sold to a three-day low yesterday, slightly below $1.3340. The broad greenback gains, and the disappointing UK January GDP provided incentive to take sterling to nearly $1.3255 today to test the low for the year set March 3. Initial resistance may be around $1.3280. The next support area is seen around $1.3200-15.

• The US dollar (DXY) broke higher against the Canadian dollar yesterday. Perhaps the deterioration of the January trade balance (the C$3.65 bln deficit) was three times larger than the median economist forecast in Bloomberg’s survey. After knocking against it for the previous three sessions, the greenback pushed convincingly above CAD1.36. It approached the lower end of a band of resistance that extends from about CAD1.3640-CAD1.3665 and edged above the upper end today, poking above CAD1.3670. The next target is around CAD1.3700-15.

• Despite continued high expectations that the Reserve Bank of Australia hikes rates next week, the Australian dollar succumbed to profit-taking. It reached the highest level since mid-2022 on Wednesday, slightly above $0.7185, and retreated to almost $0.7070 yesterday in North America and to about $0.7015 today. Options for about A$855 mln struck at $0.7000 expire today. The week’s low was recorded Monday near $0.6955.

EM

• The Mexican peso remained under pressure in the risk-off environment. The greenback reached a three-day high near MXN17.8955 and nearly MXN17.95 today. It has stabilized in Europe. Support is seen near MXN17.80. Monday’s high was around MXN18.0245. A move above MXN18.04 could signal a run toward MXN18.12-13.

• The dollar remained within Tuesday’s range (~CNH6.86-CNH6.8970) against the offshore yuan yesterday. The greenback’s broad strength spilled over and lifted it to about CNH6.9075 today and held below resistance near CNH6.91. The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate higher for the second consecutive session (CNY6.9007 vs. CNY6.8959 on Thursday). Still, it was the 14th week that the PBOC has reduced the dollar’s fix.

• The rupee fell to a new record low today despite the talk of intervention. The dollar reached almost INR92.4790. The greenback rose almost 0.8% this week after last week’s 0.85% gain.

Other Markets

• There is no relief for equities today. Nearly all the Asia Pacific markets fell. India's 2% decline was the steepest among the large bourses, though the Nikkei and Kospi fell by more than 1%. Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is off about 0.5% and came into today nearly flat on the week. US index futures are narrowly mixed.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are mostly higher. The yield on the 10-year JGB jumped around seven basis points to 2.24%. European 10-year yields are mostly firmer, though the UK Gilt yield is slightly softer, as is the German Bund. On the week, the 10-year JGB yield was flat before today’s move, and European yields are up 10-15 bp. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up a basis point at 4.27%, a 17.5 bp increase on the week. The two-year US yield is flat near 3.74%, after jumping nearly nine basis points yesterday. It settled last week at 3.56%.

• Gold is consolidating quietly around $5100. It finished last week near $5172. Silver is softer and hovers around $83 in European morning turnover. It settled around $84.50 last week.

• April WTI reached a four-day high slightly above $98 today but has slipped back toward session lows ~$94.50-$95 as the European morning progressed.

Data

• The US economic calendar is jammed today, but the war likely dampens the market’s reaction. Another look at Q4 GDP, which rose by a disappointing 1.4% at an annualized rate, may be among the least relevant as Q1 26 winds down. The most important element of the January personal income and consumption data is that, adjusted for inflation, spending be flat after rising 0.1% in December. The price deflators will remain firm, with the headline seen as stable at 2.9%, while the core may tick up to 3.1% from 3.0%. Boeing and defense orders likely flattered the January durable goods orders, without which a 0.5% gain is expected by the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey. The January JOLTS report is expected to show job openings increased for the first time in four months, while the level of quits fell for the first time in three months. This will support ideas suggested by some Fed officials that the labor market showed some signs of stabilizing recently. Lastly, it will surprise no one if the inflation expectations in the preliminary March University of Michigan survey rise. At the risk of oversimplifying, consider that the average retail price of gasoline has risen every day since the war began for a cumulative rise of more than 20%.

• Canada reports February jobs data today. It is difficult to envision a better report than January's, when the unemployment rate fell to 6.5% from 6.8%, nearly 45k jobs were created, and wage pressure eased. On February 6, when the report was released, the Canadian dollar strengthened by about 0.25%, which was broadly in line with the 0.20% decline in the Dollar Index. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey anticipates a small rise in the unemployment rate and softer wage growth.

• Mexico reports January industrial production. The Mexican economy may have avoided a recession, but the economy has yet to establish much traction. A small rise is expected.

• The eurozone reported its January industrial output figures today. Given that Germany’s decline by 0.5% and Spain by 0.4%, the median projection in Bloomberg’s survey of a 0.6% gain seemed optimistic. In fact, it slid by 1.5%, though the sting may have been lessened by the upward revision in December to -0.5% from -1.4%.

• The UK economy stagnated in January. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey was for 0.2% growth. Industrial output fell 0.1% in January (-0.9% in December), and services were flat. The construction sector grew by 0.2% (-0.5% in December). The trade balance also improved (-GBP3.9 bln vs. -GBP4.3 bln) and, excluding precious metals trade, showed a small surplus for the first time since December 2024. The Bank of England meets next week, and the odds of a cut have been slashed in the swaps market to about 5% from around 85% before the war began.

• China’s February lending figures were somewhat stronger than expected at CNY5.6 trillion in the first two months of the year. Early Monday, Beijing reports February real sector data and the economy is seen struggling to maintain much forward momentum.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Market Is Panicking Over Microsoft's AI Spending - You Shouldn't
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) continues to belie expectations and my last thesis that the stock would dominate 2026 has already been challenged.(written at the start of this year). With a lot of tech and AI stocks coming under pressure as markets re-evaluate long duration earnings assumptions, Microsoft has corrected by ~15% since the start of the year.

This correction is despite Microsoft having seen the slowest rally since the start of 2024, where most of the Magnificent Seven saw gains ranging from 50-100%. So while the rest of the tech and AI mega caps are cooling after a sharp rally in the past couple of years, Microsoft's case is not so much about rally excesses. So much so, that Microsoft is actually flat over the last couple of years (end to end).

This correction is also despite Microsoft's relatively stronger enterprise adoption layer and monetization visibility compared to most other big tech's AI investments (Copilot usage and large enterprise deployments are already scaling while margins remain stable). Traditional demand (from core non-AI segments) is also keeping operating leverage intact.

At current valuations, Microsoft is not the most expensive AI-exposed mega cap. In fact, Microsoft is trading at close to the lower end of its recent historical range. So, overall, Microsoft's long term thesis as a wealth creator is definitely intact. However, the short term thesis of an outstanding 2026 could be a long shot now. Microsoft remains a Buy, and could only suffer due to the weight of a crowded obvious trade at these levels - where the balance of scope, valuation and fundamentals show a clear asymmetry. That should not impact the long term prospects though - investors playing the long game with patience should use this opportunity (capex concern led pressures with one of the better monetization pathways) to double up on the stock.

What are Markets Worried About

After a bullish AI focused rally in 2024-25, markets are now pausing to reflect on how the demand and economics are lined up in the new era of emerging AI economics. While the demand is not the central problem, the cost to serve the demand is important. The question we are yet to understand fully is whether, unlike traditional software which is cheap to scale, AI could be extremely expensive to run. Unlike software, where incremental customer additions rely on operating leverage and not much incremental costs, AI requires massive computing power which means data center, GPU and power costs. Microsoft's ~$37.5b capex in Q2 FY2026 is not only a reflection of the scale of demand, but the amount of investments required to scale AI infrastructure. The capex run rate of ~$150b annually today is almost half the TTM revenue. The TTM capex to revenue ratio is still lagging these recent levels but still show how elevated they are compared to historical levels.

Given the demand strength and its potentially transformative size, the AI market is also not a monopoly naturally. Almost every other tech major (Amazon, Alphabet, Meta and others) are all spending heavily on AI infrastructure. This arms race situation also must have its repercussions - that is a large part of the capex spend should be attributed to simply staying competitive, a cost that increases the hurdle rate for strong profitability. Also, unlike in the case of software capex that matures over time and needs only upgrade R&D, AI infrastructure capex comes with higher depreciation, shelf lives of hardware systems, non-scalable power costs and the need for constant upgrades. The depreciation life of servers, for example, is ~6 years, while the RPO average duration is around only 2.5 years - which means revenue visibility is not comfortably covering the capex spends at the moment. There are some efficiencies that will kick in like software optimization, improved chips, etc. that will reduce cost per computation, but that is not the same thing as efficiencies that we see in software over time. The AI arms race also does not truly guarantee winners. Microsoft relies partly on partnerships with companies like OpenAI and Anthropic. Competitors have their own models too, which could become dominant and reduce Microsoft's AI play as only a hosting platform rather than a primary value creator.

Why I am Not Panicking

All the risks the markets are worried about today are valid, but are not new risks that have emerged conceptually. In fact, Microsoft has made some of the risks less vague than they were in 2024 or early 2025. We have more answers, but less market appetite for long duration assumptions. As I have mentioned in my earlier Microsoft theses, AI monetization is already happening. In Q2 FY2026, Microsoft reported 15m paid Copilot seats (up 160% YoY). The trend is extremely encouraging and shows the power of Microsoft's enormous distribution network to leverage. Even if just 20% of its Microsoft 365 commercial users (pegged at ~450m) adopt AI tools of ~$20 a month, we could see revenue additions of up to $22b annually - and that's just one product category we are talking about. Microsoft does have the firepower to monetize AI and is also showing early strength - both positives. Microsoft is already seeing pricing and product upgrade led revenue boosts. In Q2, Microsoft 365 commercial revenue grew 17% while paid seats only grew 6% - showing Microsoft's ability to increase value of each existing customer.

Another factor that should be kept in mind when attributing capex is that it is not only Azure cloud services we are talking about. The infrastructure spend supports other areas of monetization like Copilot (Microsoft 365, GitHub, Security) and enterprise AI agents.

While demand is not the core problem, it is important to note that based on the management commentary in Q2, it is clear that demand strength far exceeds supply today. Azure AI services are growing ~39% YoY and RPO is now at ~$625b (up 110% YoY).

Several trends we have been seeing across the past few quarters of elevated AI spends are comforting. One of the strongest signals that AI demand is real is the acceleration in Azure growth. There has been a clear inflection from Q4 FY2025, when YOY growth surged toward 40% from the early thirties, aided by more AI workloads moving toward production deployments. AI as a meaningful driver of cloud consumption is also apparent from the RPO surge - in Q2 roughly 45% of the increase was related to large AI infrastructure commitments, particularly from model providers. And although the ~2.5 year average commitment does not cover the infrastructure investment horizon fully, it is by itself a long term commitment, highlighting demand strength.

While the whole of FY2025 saw higher capex than usual (as seen in the capex to revenue chart we plotted before), FY2026 was a further 60-70% step up in the capex super cycle. But this period of sustained capex deployment has not yet destroyed operating margins. Through the high capex period of the past 6 quarters (and the 2 quarters of a step up in spending), operating margins have stayed remarkably stable, with 1-2 percentage point drag at worst (that too not a confirmed downtrend across at least 2-3 quarters). This does not yet prove AI infrastructure is cheap to build (because infrastructure depreciation lags capital spending, the full margin impact of the AI buildout has yet to be realized) but does indicate that early AI infrastructure spending is being offset by growing demand and the high margin nature of Microsoft's software business.

The Capex and Stress Scenarios

Rising Depreciation has already started showing up in financials in the historically aggressive capex cycle so far. In mid-2023, quarterly depreciation was roughly $3.5b, but by the most recent quarter it has reached about $9.1b. That is a $22b annual increase over the last couple of years of elevated capex. The depreciation effect is expected to be visible over the useful life of assets. Servers and AI compute infrastructure can be expected to depreciate over ~6 years, while facilities and networking infrastructure depreciate over even longer periods. If one assumes an average infrastructure life of ~6 years conservatively, this level of annualized depreciation of ~$36b (current run rate extrapolated and straight line depreciation assumed) corresponds to ~$215b of past capital investments. That is the current depreciation effects are not insignificant - they account for more than the capex reported in the past 6 quarters without disturbing operating margins meaningfully.

If the current capex run rate of ~$150b per year is sustained for several years, the depreciation base would gradually expand as new assets enter accounting while older assets roll off. Under such a stress case scenario, it is reasonable to expect depreciation to continue rising for a few years till capex and accounting effects stabilize. A simplistic but conservative assumption shows the current $36b rolling off after 6 years and $25b depreciation added every year if capex remains at $150b annually and depreciating over 6 years. This ramps up the depreciation by $25b every year and at some point the total depreciation expenses stabilize at $150b. However, if revenue were to continue growing near the company's recent 17% growth pace, even modest incremental margins (even ~30%) could eventually offset the cost ramp. In the early years depreciation would grow faster than incremental profits, but as the revenue base expands the incremental operating income begins to exceed the depreciation burden. The lower margin of ~30% is a far cry from existing margins of 45-50% and closer to current infrastructure-like margins as seen in the case of AWS (~35%).

Another way to think about the economics is through capital return thresholds. If roughly two-thirds of the annual capex (about $100b) relates to AI and cloud compute infrastructure, then even a 10% return on that infrastructure (very conservative compared to AWS's 30s) translates to $10b in earnings (~$8b assuming a 20% effective tax rate). That is ~$1.1 of incremental EPS (~7.4b shares outstanding) for a single year of capital deployment. The impact of a multi year capex deployment and even weak AI returns would mean the EPS expected in FY2028 could jump from ~$13.6 in FY2025 to $17 even in a very weak return scenario. And given valuations are at historically low levels, even if markets consider the low return result a case for downgrading even further to ~22x earnings valuations, the FY2028 target for the stock becomes $374 (not far from today's levels) and showing a very solid floor even in a very weak case. Importantly, this stress framework assumes the core Microsoft business generates no incremental earnings growth over the next three years. It assumes no AI pricing power almost or efficiency improvements.

A Strong Buy

Microsoft at current valuations look like a Strong Buy for long term investors. That valuations are near historical lows at a time when the opportunity ahead is one of the largest seen in years makes for an asymmetrical risk reward setup. The risks to the thesis is a prolonged slump because the uncertainties of the economics of the AI ecosystem is really a factor and markets are certainly not in a mood to reward long duration assumptions. But it is that very setup that provides an edge. To me a Buy looks so obvious that apart from the AI economics (which still seems to suggest a solid floor) the crowded Buy trade on the stock is its biggest risk. That should not matter for investors who have time on their side.

My earlier outlook of Microsoft having a strong run in 2026 is a dim possibility now and we may see a slow grind up from here, although I will not be surprised if there is a sharp reversal too, especially is market appetite returns. Microsoft valuations, a bull run that is due from the past couple of years (compared to the rest of the AI mega caps) and the fact that it is already monetizing AI with encouraging trends makes for a great opportunity to accumulate at the recent lows.

data.title: Renault: Favorable Takeaways From Strategy Day
data.description:
I've kept Renault SA (RNSDF)(RNLSY)(RNO.FP) as a buy-rated name.

RNSDF hosted a "Strategy Day" branded as "futuREady" (S&P Capital IQ transcript) on Tuesday, March 10, 2026. Disclosures from this event have made me more confident about its prospects.

If you were considering a potential investment in Renault, its favorable margin and dividend outlook would have made a strong case for its attractiveness. A 50bps EBIT-to-revenue expansion is expected in the coming years. The stock should be able to maintain a high single-digit dividend yield, considering the capital returns policy.

The company's above-consensus 3Q25 topline and potential H2 financial improvement were outlined in my earlier October 27, 2025 write-up.

Management Reaffirms Key Goals

At February's 4Q25 results briefing, RNSDF disclosed its aim to register a "5% to 7% operating profit margin (OPM)" for "the midterm." The firm also committed to "deliver every year a EUR 400 cost reduction per vehicle."

The above-mentioned objectives were echoed at "futuREady" this week. I'm encouraged by the continued focus on profitability.

The group's FY2026 EBIT-to-sales guide is 5.5%. As per S&P Capital IQ, analysts are projecting that OPM will improve to 5.8% and 6.0% in FY28 and FY29, respectively. This is consistent with RNSDF's aspirations.

Also, the enterprise's Q4 call commentary emphasized its shareholder return policy involving a "progressive increase in dividend per share." That will be supported by the targeted yearly "Free Cash Flow/FCF" expansion from 2026's €1.0B to €1.5B over the intermediate-term. It's reassuring for me to learn that these remain unchanged at the recent investor event.

The stock's trailing dividend-to-price is 7.6% based on an FY25 payout of EUR 2.2/share. Its forward yields look even more appealing with the assumption of growing cash distributions.

Multiple Levers For Margin Accretion And Cash Flow Generation

My take is that RNSDF's targets detailed in the preceding section are achievable. This takes into account the Strategy Day's takeaways.

I think its future profitability should be boosted by higher turnover and the resulting fixed-cost leverage.

RNSDF plans to introduce thirty-six new nameplates between now and the end of this decade. Nearly two-fifths of the pipeline is dedicated to launches in non-European markets.

The group forecasts that its Renault-branded vehicle sales will rise to over 2M on an annual basis in '30. About half of the volume is anticipated to be contributed by high-growth foreign regions like Brazil and India then. That translates into a decent five-year CAGR of +4%, according to my calculations.

The €400 per-unit production expense cut is realistic in my opinion.

RNSDF revealed at "futuREady" that its "new RGEV Medium 2.0 platform" can shorten the EV development timeline from 3-5 years to 24 months. It also highlighted proposals to install "350 humanoid robots in our plants and lower the number of parts per vehicle by 30%."

Separately, the firm intends to be very conservative in the way it invests. Capital allocated to R&D and CapEx every year as a proportion of top-line won't exceed 8 percent going forward.

I've identified a new cash flow stream emerging in FY27. RNSDF owned a 45% stake in a "HORSE Powertrain thermal engines joint venture." At the March investor event, management mentioned HORSE's "two-year (2025-2026) standstill for dividends." It guided for "significant" payouts from this JV next year.

RNSDF also sees its finance subsidiary, "Mobilize Financial Services'/MFS'" annual cash distribution rising from FY2025's €0.3B to €0.5B for the medium-term. It was previously noted at the 4Q call that "the capital situation at MFS is strengthened" with an "equity raise last year."

In my view, there are a number of drivers presented above supporting the EUR 1,500M free cash flow goal.

Also, I believe RNSDF is turning into a more stockholder-centric entity. It stressed at "futuREady" the "value shared with our shareholders" and the need to "deliver fair returns." The current high-single-digit dividend yield appears to be sustainable.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

RNSDF may fall short of its intended margin enhancement. This can happen because of negative operating leverage linked to weaker-than-anticipated demand. There's no guarantee that the new products will be well-received by customers.

This name could be out of favor with yield-hunting investors. A potential bear-case scenario is an unexpected dividend cut. Management can possibly change their minds and decide to set aside capital for inorganic initiatives.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm still bullish on this stock.

RNSDF is now trading at 4.2x next twelve months' earnings, as per S&P Capital IQ.

I stick to the "6x P/E (all-time historical average)" valuation benchmark used in my Oct '25 piece. That translates into a capital appreciation potential of +43% (6.0/4.2).

Better profit margins and more generous payouts should trigger a positive re-rating of RNSDF's shares in time to come. This warrants my 'buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brace For 'Sharply Higher Rates' - The FOMC Meeting Preview
data.description:
The Fed's March meeting

The FOMC is set to meet on March 17-18th, and it's widely expected to leave the Federal Funds rate unchanged at the 3.5-3.75% range, midpoint 3.66%.

The FOMC will also release the updated Summary of Economic Projections (SEP), which will signal the future policy path over the next two years.

Based on the most recent SEP from December 2025, the Fed signaled one additional cut in 2026 to 3.4% and one additional cut in 2027 to 3.1%, which is considered to be the neutral rate.

Thus, the Fed expects to continue with a gradual policy normalization until 2027, at which point 1) inflation will be near the 2% target, 2) the unemployment rate will be at full employment, and 3) GDP growth will be at full potential - that's pure Goldilocks.

Tell this story to the stock market, and obviously the stock market will love it. However, the reality is likely to be much different, as there are many unfolding events that could cause 1) a deflationary recession 2) as well as an inflationary recession. Currently, we are facing an inflationary shock.

Inflationary global adverse shock unfolding

The Fed publishes an annual Severe Adverse Scenario to stress-test the banks and the overall financial system. As a recently warned, for 2026 the Fed is bracing for global stagflation:

2026 Global Market Shock Component of the Supervisory Severely Adverse Scenario

The 2026 global market shock is characterized by heightened market expectations of persistently high inflation, higher commodity prices, and a global recession.

The scenario has certain elements in common with prior episodes of market reactions to periods of expected high inflation combined with low growth, such as the oil crisis of the 1970s. That period was also characterized by commodity price increases.

Both short-term and long-term Treasury rates rise sharply driven by higher inflation expectations.

Heightened inflation expectations drive commodity prices upward.

The expected fall in economic activity leads to notable equity price declines across global markets.

Concerns about corporate credit defaults in light of the economic slowdown leads to wider credit spreads.

The U.S. dollar strengthens, exhibiting large gains against the euro and moderate gains against the Japanese yen driven by higher yields in the U.S.

The current war with Iran exactly fits the Fed's Global Severely Adverse Scenario - the price of oil is near $100, and if it remains near or above $100 for a few months, we will have a similar situation to the Fed's referenced period, "the oil crisis of the 1970s."

So, what will the Fed do in this scenario? Well, they tell us in the quote above, "short-term rates rise sharply" - that means the Fed will have to "sharply" increase interest rates, just like they did in the 1970s.

It's all about the oil now

Thus, the current macro scenario is simple:

  • The war with Iran pushes oil above $100, and the price stays there for an extended period of time, possibly for several months up to a year (3-12 months).
  • A persistently high oil price causes an inflationary shock, as the price of gas increases, as well as the cost of transportation, food, and many other oil-related byproducts.
  • In this situation, the Fed has to follow the playbook and hike aggressively, if not, the US Dollar (DXY) collapses, which would cause a much bigger problem.

So, the key here is how long the oil price shock will last. Brent crude oil is already above $100 - how long will it remain near the level? That depends on how long the war lasts and, specifically, how long Iran wants the war to last.

Current war dynamics are as follows:

  • Iran's strategy is to cause a global stagflationary shock via the oil price shock. Thus, Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, and it's targeting the oil infrastructure in the region (oil fields, terminals, ports, storages, and refineries). Iran can do this as long as they have the missiles, drones, and mines to attack - which could be a long time.
  • To stop the campaign, Iran demanded that the regional countries close the US bases in the area, with guarantees that Iran would not be attacked again, and even asked for repatriation for war damages.
  • The US and Israel need to secure Iran's stockpile of enriched uranium, at minimum, and preferably have a "more flexible" regime for future negotiations.

Bottom line, currently both sides are far from reaching a cease-fire that would reopen Hormuz and ease the oil price shock. Thus, at this point, it looks like the Fed's global adverse shock scenario is inevitable.

However, President Trump and his team are aware of this scenario and likely doing everything in their power to ease the price of rice oil over the near term, which includes the global coordinated release of strategic petroleum reserves.

It is also likely that President Trump will declare the victory that has been achieved on the battlefield, even if the key objective (uranium) has not been achieved. This would likely end the war, and oil supplies would be gradually restored.

Implications for the Fed

The war just started, and inflation data will not reflect the high oil price (and all other related commodities/products) for at least a month. Thus, at this point data becomes irrelevant, and it's all about the price of oil.

Thus, the Fed is likely to:

  • Acknowledge uncertainty with respect to inflation, and commit to the wait-and-see approach, which means a signal of no cuts in 2026, and this is already being priced in the Federal Funds futures. The current implied Federal Funds rate for December 2026 is 3.48%, and it was at 3.1% before the war.
  • Signal the need to hike interest rates should the oil price shock persist, and this will likely be at the post-meeting press conference.

Implications for investors

The Fed tells us that if the global adverse scenario becomes reality, we can expect:

  • "Sharply higher long-term rates" (US10Y) (TLT) - 10Y yields have been rising, with 10Y Breakeven Inflation at 2.40%, and the problems start when it crosses 2.5%.
  • "Notable decline in equity prices - the S&P 500 has been remarkably stable (SP500) but showing vulnerability when oil crosses $100, so that's the pain threshold.
  • Stronger Dollar: The US is energy independent, but Europe and Asia are likely to suffer much more.

Likely outcome

It difficult to see how President Trump would allow the global inflationary recession to happen right before the midterm elections. My opinion is that the war will end within a week or two. Still, the price of oil (CL1:COM) is likely to remain high enough to prevent the Fed from cutting in 2026, especially if the labor market remains resilient. However, until this happens, we need to brace for higher rates and lower stock prices.

data.title: 2 Powerful Dividend Growth Machines Down 30-50%: Only 1 Is A Buy
data.description:
While the S&P 500 (SPY) still hovers near all-time highs and trades at a level that some prominent investors are referring to as a bubble, and investors like Howard Marks are cautioning that it could result in a lost decade ahead, there are some powerful dividend growth machines that have dropped firmly into bear market territory, presenting compelling potential buying opportunities. In today's article, I want to look at two of these that I have not discussed in quite some time and determine if the sell-off is warranted or if the market has handed investors a golden buying opportunity.

Alternative Asset Management Giant At A Discount

The first one I am going to discuss is Brookfield Asset Management (BAM). It is trading well off its 52-week high of $64.10 as of this writing, as it trades at $43.13, offering investors a next 12 months dividend yield of about 4.5%.

BAM is a capital-light alternative asset manager with over $1.2 trillion in total assets under management and $603 billion in fee-bearing capital. While its parent, Brookfield Corporation (BN), combines a stake in the asset management business with direct ownership of substantial stakes in the assets it manages, BAM employs an asset-light business model.

This asset-light business model enables it to earn very high returns on invested capital through the growth of assets under management without having to deploy much in the way of retained earnings. Instead, it generates a ton of free cash flow that it is able to return to shareholders via dividends and buybacks. This was witnessed most recently through its 15% dividend increase that it announced in its latest earnings call.

In fact, it pays out the vast majority of its cash flows as dividends, as evidenced by its current 95% payout ratio. It can afford to do this since it does not need to retain cash to invest in capital expenditures or even to service a significant amount of debt, and therefore can pass through its cash flows directly to shareholders.

Durable Assets and Growth Tailwinds

Given how strongly it is growing and the fact that analysts forecast a 16% distributable earnings per share compound annual growth rate through 2027, which also happens to align with management expectations, the 4.5% next 12-month dividend yield looks very compelling.

Additionally, BAM's assets enjoy powerful growth tailwinds as it is leaning into multi-trillion-dollar megatrends such as digitalization, deglobalization, and decarbonization.

In fact, despite its credit exposure being quite high, a significant amount of this credit is actually in its other core pillars of infrastructure, renewable power, and real estate. This is an important distinction to make because the market has been beating down BAM along with other alternative asset managers like Blackstone (BX), Ares Management (ARES), Apollo Global Management (APO), and several others, largely due to concerns about private credit and, in particular, software loans.

However, BAM has limited exposure to direct lending relative to these other peers that are much more heavily exposed to it, and BAM has de minimis exposure to software loans as it instead focuses primarily on real asset equity and credit investing.

Additionally, its balance sheet is very strong, with $3 billion in corporate liquidity and an A-minus credit rating from S&P, which further reduces risks. Overall, it expects to double its business over the next five years and continue to grow at a rapid clip beyond that as well.

The AI Threat To Traditional Tax Services

The other opportunity is H&R Block (HRB), which offers an even higher next 12-month dividend yield of 5.7%, which is also on the high end of its historical average. Not only that, but it is down more than 50% from its 52-week high as it sits at $30.22 today compared to its 52-week high of $64.62. However, there is a greater justification in my view for it to be trading down relative to BAM, despite its 9.4% analyst expected earnings per share compound annual growth rate through 2027.

Moreover, management recently reaffirmed its fiscal year 2026 outlook, while also buying back a lot of stock. In fact, over the past five years alone, HRB has reduced its shares outstanding by over 30%:

The reason for my pessimism is that there are very real risks to the business model. In particular, there is concern that artificial intelligence will disrupt the business due to the fact that it can handle complex tax issues.

What is HRB's business model? It is a leading tax preparation and small business services company that prides itself on combining its vast network of local branches with a strong reputation for expertise and friendly human-to-human service, which it is augmenting with technology, including AI.

While HRB combines convenience with a human touch, AI can potentially completely replace part of its business and lead to a plethora of cheaper startups threatening its human-facing business, thereby eating away at its moat and its profit margins.

Management is trying to argue against this bear case by pointing to the fact that 55% of the population still wants to speak with other people when it comes to taxes. AI will simply make their business more efficient by helping their tax professionals work more quickly and better serve their clients. In fact, management went to great lengths on its latest earnings call to elaborate on how HRB is already implementing AI tools into its workflows. However, even if AI is an actual help for the business in the near term by cutting costs, over the next five years, it is hard to know where HRB will be. In particular, it is difficult for me to see how they will have much of a moat, as AI will likely lead to numerous startups challenging their business.

Navigating Uncertainty With Capital Allocation

In the meantime, HRB is pumping out a lot of cash, so the steep decline in its share price is an opportunity for it to buy back stock aggressively. This is thereby helping to increase intrinsic value per share and support its dividend growth today (following its 12% hike last year) in the event that its business is eaten into tomorrow by AI.

Not only that, but HRB is diversifying into small business services that it expects to have higher margins and more stickiness than individual refocused tax prep. However, even that business could be quite sensitive to AI disruption.

Investor Takeaway

When it comes to deciding whether or not either or both of these is a buy, I think BAM is clearly a buy, given its very strong growth outlook and the fact that it is riding major macro tailwinds like the energy transition and the AI and infrastructure booms, as well as its exposure to premier real estate properties and leading cities around the world that should be able to stand the test of time.

Meanwhile, HRB is likely going to be hit hard by AI at some point in the coming years, while it does have a strong position of incumbency that should help it to stay ahead of the competition for a while. Today, it is gushing cash that it can use to front-load returns to shareholders. I simply have much less confidence in it over the long term. Yes, it is very cheap, so some of this AI disruption is priced in, but I still view it as a very speculative bet. Thus, I am steering clear of HRB and instead have just started to buy BAM on its recent dip, in my 7-8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market-beta portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.description:
Objective

High total return through its target retirement date

Use for

Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced solid returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund (JHRLX) posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. The fund’s overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States also contributed. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outpaced its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund’s active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund’s positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it’s impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

data.title: CareTrust REIT: This Overlooked REIT's Hot Streak Continues, But The Bargain Is Gone (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

CareTrust REIT (CTRE) is a REIT I've been bullish on for the past few years. Even in the high-interest-rate environment, CTRE has continued to outperform. And their forward multiple is a direct reflection of their performance.

As a result, upside may be minimal, and this is the reason I'm downgrading the stock from buy to hold. However, lower interest rates could provide tailwinds, leading to accelerated investment activity in 2026 and beyond.

And this could push the stock price higher. In their latest quarter, CTRE ended the year with double-digit increases in their top and bottom lines.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, 2026 outlook, and why I'm downgrading shares from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered CareTrust REIT this past December in an article titled: The REIT That's Outperforming the S&P.

At the time, CTRE had managed to outperform the S&P in the past 1, 3, 5, and 10 years in total returns, debunking the myth that REITs were chronic underperformers.

Since then, CTRE has continued to see price appreciation, up more than 5%, in comparison to the S&P (SP500), down a little over 1% at the time of writing.

During that time, CareTrust REIT appeared overvalued as they sat well above their normal multiple. As a result, I suggested investors look for a pullback in the stock around $32 or below for a margin of safety.

But because of their performance and higher growth, I agreed with Wall Street analysts and thought the stock could see more upside closer to their price target of $39.55. I assumed the REIT could trade anywhere between 18x and 20x earnings going forward.

Another Record Year

CareTrust REIT reported Q4 earnings with FFO in line with analysts' estimates and a beat on revenue. FFO amounted to $0.47, while revenue of $134.86 million beat by $4.41 million.

Although FFO was in line, CTRE's performance was impressive as the REIT saw strong growth from the year prior. On a per share basis, normalized FFO grew 17.3%. In total, this grew 42.7% to $104.1 million.

This was driven by the REIT's strong investment activity and 2.5% annual rent escalators. Investments surpassed the record they set in 2024. For 2025, acquisitions totaled $1.8 billion, up from $1.5 billion.

During the quarter, management made $562 million in investments at a blended yield of 8.8%.

Subsequent to the quarter's end, CTRE closed on an additional 6 properties for $215 million. Their pipeline was strong at $500 million, positioning the REIT well for a lower rate environment.

International Expansion Remains a Focus

Focusing on expanding internationally seems to be a key growth driver for CTRE. In Q4, the REIT continued to diversify away from skilled nursing facilities by acquiring 2 care homes in the U.K.

Of their pipeline, half of these are investments in the U.K., with 1/3 being SNFs and a small SHOP deal.

Skilled nursing facilities still make up the largest portion of the portfolio at 366 properties. At quarter's end, occupancy was 79.2%. The total property count totaled 566, up from 406 the year prior.

Dividend & Balance Sheet

For 2025, CTRE's strong acquisition activity also enabled strong funds available for distribution growth, or FAD. Some REITs report this as AFFO. During the quarter, FAD amounted to $0.46, up 12.2% from Q4'24.

In total, this grew an impressive 38.7% to $103 million. As a result, this gives CTRE the flexibility to continue adding to their 11-year streak of consecutive dividend increases.

I also suspect they could raise the dividend by double digits again, like they did last year. In 2024, they raised the dividend over 15% to $0.3350. In the next month or so I anticipate another dividend increase in the $0.36 - $0.38 range.

The company ended 2025 with a payout ratio of 74%. Their balance sheet continues to be one of the most impressive I've seen among REITs.

Net debt to EBITDA was just 0.7x, up from 0.5x the year prior. Their fixed-charge coverage ratio was 10.5x. Liquidity was robust with $100 million in cash and $1.2 billion untapped on their revolver.

Their next debt maturity isn't until 2028, with $400 million due. This gives CTRE plenty of room for strong acquisition activity in the coming years.

Outlook

For 2026, CTRE's management expects FFO & FAD in a range of $1.90 - $1.95. This represents a growth rate of 9.4% from 2025. While impressive growth, especially for a REIT, this is lower than the 17.3% they experienced in 2025.

FAD growth was a tad lower at 14.3%. While this could be a signal that CTRE's growth is slowing, this excludes any investment activity and dispositions. I suspect they could see 15% to 20% growth if M&A activity increases.

In 2025, the REIT grew their market 61% to $8.2 billion. In 2024, this was higher at 74%, to $5.1 billion. So, despite the challenging macro environment, CTRE showed no signs of slowing down. And going forward, the lower interest rate environment could allow for more of the same.

Valuation Looks Stretched

At a price of $40, CareTrust REIT appears fully valued here. In my previous article, I stated I thought the company could trade between 18x and 20x, fair for a company of their caliber.

Using their guidance midpoint, this gives them a forward P/FFO multiple of approximately 21.0x. Despite their quality, I think this is a bit stretched for a REIT. I personally like them in the mid-$30 range or lower.

For context, this is significantly higher than a REIT I consider to be one of the highest quality, Agree Realty (ADC). Although CTRE focuses on healthcare, ADC's 67% investment-grade tenant concentration and A-rated balance sheet warrant a high premium.

At the current price of $78, this gives them a forward P/AFFO multiple of 17.76x using their midpoint of $4.56. Omega Healthcare Investors' (OHI) forward P/AFFO multiple sits at 14.71x. Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) had the lowest at 12.71x.

While CTRE could have some upside left, the dividend yield of 3.35% is less attractive. I do think lower yields on fixed-rate investments could be a catalyst, as REITs could benefit from lower interest rates. But it's likely investors will look for higher yields, likely 4% or higher.

Risks & Conclusion

Besides an overall weaker economy, CTRE's management stated during Q4 they were seeing increased competition in SHOP. Cap rates were compressed due to more investors flocking to the sector seeking exposure.

But the company's diversification into the U.K. puts them in a comfortable position to navigate headwinds.

Going forward, investors should keep an eye on occupancy trends, as declines could result in lower financials. If so, this could lead to share price volatility.

Furthermore, the recent spike in long-term treasuries could be a signal of inflation reaccelerating, particularly due to the U.S.-Iran War. If this continues, CTRE's shares could pull back as a result.

All in all, I continue to remain bullish on CareTrust REIT due to their low leverage, robust liquidity, and astute management.

The REIT continues to outperform, driven by double-digit growth. And while a lower base rate could accelerate this, leading to additional capital appreciation, their valuation appears stretched, leading me to downgrade them to a hold.
